coredata.dc:title: DNAzyme amplified dispersion state change of gold nanoparticles and its dual optical channels for ultrasensitive and facile detection of lead ion in preserved eggs
coredata.dc:creator: Yan, Wenju
Qin, Xuefei
Sang, Xueqing
Zhou, Xueying
Zheng, Yanying
Yuan, Yali
Zhang, Yun
coredata.dc:description: A dual-mode sensing platform for Pb2+ was constructed based on the dual optical channels of Au NPs system with the amplification of DNAzyme, and it was successfully applied for Pb2+ determination in preserved egg with satisfactory results. The presence of Pb2+ activated the DNAzyme and induced the dispersion change of Au NPs in high salt concentration. The sequent absorption change of Au NPs was translated to the fluorescence change of carbon dots through FRET, and the scattering change was transferred to grey value of images involving the Tyndall effect. Thus, a sensing platform based on fluorescence and colorimetric dual-technique was achieved for Pb2+ detection, under the optimized conditions. With the assistance of DNAzyme, the linear range of fluorometric and colorimetric method were 2.0 × 10-14 ∼ 8.0 × 10-10 mol/L and 2.4 × 10-13 ∼ 9.5 × 10-9 mol/L, respectively. The dual-mode sensing platform demonstrated its promising application in the environmental monitoring and food safety field.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Gold nanoparticles
Fluorescence and colorimetric dual optical channel
Carbon dots
Tyndall effect
Lead ion

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of colloidal Au NPs and CDs
Characterization of synthesized Au NPs and CDs
Feasibility of Pb2+ detection through dual optical channels of Au NPs system
Optimization of experimental conditions in dual channels
Pb2+ detection performance of dual modes

National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 22364011), Central Government-Guided Local Science and Technology Development Project (No. GuikeZY20198006), and Guangxi Natural Science Foundation (2020GXNSFBA297116).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) UV–Vis absorption spectrum and (B) TEM image of AuNPs (Inset: DLS of Au NPs). (C) TEM and (D) HRTEM images of CDs. Inset in (C) illustrates the size distribution of CDs. (D) of AuNPs. (E) UV–Vis absorption spectrum of CDs (Inset: 3D fluorescence spectrum of CDs). (F) FT-IR spectrum of CDs. XPS survey spectra of CDs: (G) C1s, (H) O1s, and (I) N1s.

(A) UV–Vis absorption spectra of reaction system without (blank) and with (sample) the presence of Pb2+. Inset: the photos of blank and sample solution. TEM images of AuNPs in absence (B) and presence (C) of Pb2+. (D) TE photos of blank and sample solutions.

(A) Excitation and emission fluorescence spectra of CDs and UV–Vis absorption spectrum of Au NPs. (B) Fluorescence spectra of reaction solutions with and without the presence of Pb2+, and fluorescence spectrum of CDs solution. (C) Influence of various species with different concentrations on fluorescence intensity of CDs. (D) Zeta potential of CDs and Au NPs. (E) UV–Vis absorption of Au NPs, CDs, and the mixture of Au NPs and CDs. (F) Fluorescence lifetime of CDs, and CDs mixing with AuNPs.

(A) TE photos and ΔMGV value recorded from the assays of a series of sample solutions containing different concentration of Pb2+. Inset: linear plot of ΔMGV and logarithm of Pb2+ concentration. (B) Colorimetric images and UV–Vis absorption spectra of sample solutions containing different concentration of Pb2+. Inset: linear relationship between the ratio of absorbance at 520 nm and 650 nm and logarithm of Pb2+ concentration. (C) Fluorescence curves of sample solutions with different concentration of Pb2+. Inset: magnified fluorescence curves in the range of 510–550 nm. (D) The change of fluorescence intensity of sample solutions with the Pb2+ concentration. Inset: linear plot of fluorescence intensity and logarithm of Pb2+ concentration. (E) ΔMGV value and (F) fluorescence intensity obtained from the blank solution (without Pb2+), sample solution with 100 pmol/L Pb2+ and other metal ions (1 nmol/L).
(A) Schematic diagram of dual-channel detection of lead ion. (B) The treating process of preserved egg samples.
Recovery of Pb2+ in preserved egg samples by fluorescent and TE channel.

head.ce:title: DNAzyme amplified dispersion state change of gold nanoparticles and its dual optical channels for ultrasensitive and facile detection of lead ion in preserved eggs

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Guangxi Key Laboratory of Electrochemical and Magneto-chemical Functional Materials, Guilin University of Technology, 12 Jiangan Road, Guilin 541004, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guangxi Key Laboratory of Electrochemical and Magneto-chemical Functional Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Guangxi Key Laboratory of Electrochemical and Magneto-chemical Functional Materials, Guilin University of Technology, 12 Jiangan Road, Guilin 541004, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Bioengineering, Guilin University of Technology, 12 Jiangan Road, Guilin 541004, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Bioengineering, Guilin University of Technology, 12 Jiangan Road, Guilin 541004, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A dual-mode sensing platform for Pb2+ was constructed based on the dual optical channels of Au NPs system with the amplification of DNAzyme, and it was successfully applied for Pb2+ determination in preserved egg with satisfactory results. The presence of Pb2+ activated the DNAzyme and induced the dispersion change of Au NPs in high salt concentration. The sequent absorption change of Au NPs was translated to the fluorescence change of carbon dots through FRET, and the scattering change was transferred to grey value of images involving the Tyndall effect. Thus, a sensing platform based on fluorescence and colorimetric dual-technique was achieved for Pb2+ detection, under the optimized conditions. With the assistance of DNAzyme, the linear range of fluorometric and colorimetric method were 2.0 × 10-14 ∼ 8.0 × 10-10 mol/L and 2.4 × 10-13 ∼ 9.5 × 10-9 mol/L, respectively. The dual-mode sensing platform demonstrated its promising application in the environmental monitoring and food safety field.

ce:keyword.ce:text: Fluorescence and colorimetric dual optical channel

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: With the development of human society and industrial manufacture, heavy metal pollution has become a severe threat to our ecosystem (Shin, Yu, Wang, Jeong, & Kim, 2020), which has aroused extensive public concern in recent decades. As one of the most hazardous heavy metal pollutants, lead is poisonous due to the nature of non-biodegradable, carcinogenicity and organism accumulation (Botte, Seguin, Nahrgang, Zaidi, Guery, & Leignel, 2022). Lead exposure is linked with high blood pressure, kidney disorders, behavioral abnormalities, decline in organ function and nervous system diseases, and to make things even worse, these diseases will affect health of the next generation (Botte, Seguin, Nahrgang, Zaidi, Guery, & Leignel, 2022). Therefore, it is of great importance to monitor the lead level continuously in environment and food with high sensitivity and selectivity. At present, traditional detection techniques, such as inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry(Fan et al., 2023), atomic absorption (de Andrade, de Brito, dos Anjos, & Quináia, 2018) and atomic fluorescence spectrometry (Feng, Liu, Mao, Lu, Zhu, & Qian, 2016) are usually taken as standard methods for Pb2+ detection, whereas the requirement of complex and expensive instrumentation, professional operation, and cumbersome sample pretreatment hinder their advanced application. To address these issues, other techniques, such as fluorescence (Talio, Munoz, Acosta, & Fernandez, 2019), electrochemistry (Yang et al., 2019), chemiluminescence (Xu, Suo, Zhang, Li, Chen, & Zhang, 2020) and colorimetry (Nguyen, Sung, O'Shaughnessy, Shan, & Shih, 2018) have emerged with ingenious designs in ease of Pb2+ detection, thanks to the assistance of diverse nanomaterials (Lv, Wang, Wu, Li, & Li, 2019; Sang et al., 2022).

Gold nanoparticles (Au NPs), a kind of traditional yet popular star nanomaterial, have received great attention in various aspects due to their excellent electron transfer ability, low toxicity, admirable biocompatibility and tunable optical properties (Qin, Lu, Bian, Xiao, Zhang, & Yuan, 2019; Xiao, Meng, Qin, Sang, Zhang, & Yuan, 2021). When involved in sensing platform, Au NPs commonly act the roles of 1) enhancing element, such as in the surface plasmon resonance (SPR) enhanced spectroscopy (Fathi, Rashidi, & Omidi, 2019) and electrochemistry (Yang et al., 2019), or modification substrate for signal amplification (Wang et al., 2022); and 2) signal emitter, especially optical probe(Qin, Lu, Bian, Xiao, Zhang, & Yuan, 2019), which is extensively studied in numerous reports. As the optical probe, one of the unique optical properties of Au NPs is SPR, which has a strong absorption band due to the extremely high molar extinction coefficient(Huang, Li, Pan, Wang, Liang, & Jiang, 2021). The LSPR of Au NPs is strongly relied on particle size, geometry, refractive index of the medium and distance between particles(Woiski et al., 2017; Bai, Gu, Liu, Gai & Li, 2023), and tuning particle size is the common strategy to change the LSPR of Au NPs.

The most used and convenient method to achieve particle size change is to induce the disaggregation or aggregation state of Au NPs, which exhibits the color change between red and blue and hence is the common mechanism of the colorimetric approaches based on Au NPs (Bian, Xu, Yuan & Nie, 2020). Given the notable absorption band of Au NPs, they are used as effective quencher of various luminophores in diverse detection strategies via fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) (Zhang, Gong, Guo, Zhang, & Ding, 2018) or inner filter effect (IFE)(Qin, Lu, Bian, Xiao, Zhang, & Yuan, 2019). Thus the absorption of Au NPs in different dispersion state can be not only applied as the quantitative data in colorimetric system, but furthermore can be converted to fluorescence signal combing with luminescent donors. Compared with the UV–Vis technique used in colorimetric analysis, the fluorescent method provides higher sensitivity and greater diversity of detection strategies with the aid of multiple fluorescent probes. For example, carbon dots (CDs), an emerging and promising class of fluorescent nanomaterials, is of great potential for developing sensors for different detection aims(Qin et al., 2022).

When the dispersion state (i.e. particle size) changes, another optical phenomenon of Au NPs-scattering, is also affected. The scattering light intensity of Au NPs is about 3–6 orders higher than that of an organic fluorescing dye and 2–3 orders higher than that of a polymer bead(Jain et al., 2006), which make Au NPs the ideal nanomaterial for scattering application. As surface enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) based biosensing(Turzhitsky et al., 2018) and backscattering based bioimaging(Chen et al., 2013) have been widely studied, Rayleigh scattering and Mie scattering based Tyndall effect (TE)(Chen et al., 2021) is rarely discussed in sensing field, even though it is quite common in our daily life. According to the fact that Au NPs in larger size show stronger scattering efficiency than Au NPs in smaller size(Jain et al., 2006), the dispersion state change between aggregation and disaggregation can induce observable change of Tyndall effect. Lit up with a simple laser pointer, the Tyndall effect image of Au NPs system is recorded by a smartphone and the gray value is taken as the quantitative data. Leveraging this easy procedure, a facile and instrument-free detection platform can be constructed. Our group have made the first trials in this field and have already achieved satisfactory results for certain analysis targets(Deng et al., 2021).

To obtain the analyte-induced aggregation change of Au NPs, one of the easiest ways is employing various biological molecules, including DNA and proteins, to functionalize and stabilize Au NPs, and the protection effect can be strongly affected by the addition of analyte. Due to the high affinity of single-stranded DNA (ssDNA) onto Au NPs surface through electrostatic interaction, the analyte-induced conformation change of DNA, such as from ssDNA to dsDNA (Ma, Li, Xue, Cheng, & Miao, 2018), or folded DNA to free and short chains(Yan et al., 2015), are frequently involved in different analytical methods. To improve the sensitivity of assays, DNAzyme technique(Huang et al., 2019), associated with both DNA and signal amplification, is considered as excellent candidate for the amplified biosensing. The DNAzyme is comprised of an enzyme strand (E-DNA) and a substrate strand (S-DNA). Since the catalytic activity of DNAzyme generally requires metal ions as cofactors to be triggered (Huang, de Rochambeau, Sleiman, & Liu, 2020), DNAzyme have been widely utilized for constructing various metal ions responsive biosensors, such as Pb2+, Zn2+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Ca2+, Cu2+, and Na+ (Lake, Yang, Zhang, & Lu, 2019).

Herein, based on the aforementioned dual optical channels of Au NPs system, a dual-mode sensing platform for Pb2+ was constructed with the amplification of DNAzyme. As shown in Scheme 1 A, the Pb2+-dependent DNAzyme is designed to comprise an E-DNA and a modified hairpin structure S-DNA. When partially hybridizing with E-DNA, the stem of S-DNA can be opened and form intact DNAzyme structure. Without the addition of Pb2+, the DNAzyme with double stranded conformation has little protection toward Au NPs in high salt concentration, leading to the aggregation of Au NPs. On the contrast, in the presence of Pb2+, the DNAzyme is activated to cleavage S-DNA into two chains. These short chains can be released from DNAzyme and bind on the surface of Au NPs to stabilize them against the salt-induced aggregation. Meanwhile, the liberated E-DNA can hybridize with another S-DNA, and continues to cleavage S-DNA with the assistance of Pb2+, repeating the cycle of releasing ssDNA into the system to protect Au NPs. Hence, the difference between aggregation and disaggregation of Au NPs induced by Pb2+ is amplified and transmitted to distinct phenomena in absorption and scattering. In the scattering channel, the aggregation of Au NPs, i.e. in the absence of Pb2+, produces strong TE under the irradiation of a simple laser pointer. In the channel of absorption, CDs are introduced as the luminophore. The absorption peak of aggregated Au NPs has less overlap with the fluorescence emission peak of CDs, giving rise to stronger fluorescence intensity than that of disaggregated Au NPs. Therefore, the evaluation of Pb2+ concentration is accomplished based on the dual signals of TE and fluorescence, which are able to provide complementary and verified result and yield a wider linear range. The analysis of Pb2+ in preserved egg is also successfully carried out (Scheme 1B). Another advantage of this proposed strategy is that the range of analysis targets can be considerably expanded by easily tuning the sequence of DNA to other DNAzyme or aptamers.

Chloroauric acid tetrahydrate (HAuCl4·4H2O), aniline, ethanediamine and urea were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. The standard solution of lead nitrate (Pb(NO3)2) was bought from Zhongbiao Technology (Jinan) Co., Ltd. Mercury nitrate, copper chloride, and other metal salts were obtained from Aladdin Biochem Technology (Shanghai) Co. Ltd. S-DNA (5′-CAC CAC AAA ACT CAC TAT rAG GAA GAG ATG ATA AAG TGG TG −3′) and E-DNA (5′-ATC ATC TCT GAA GTA GCG CCG CCG TAT AGT GAG −3′) were purchased from Sangon Biotechnology (Shanghai) Co., Ltd. All other chemicals were of analytical grade and used without further purification. Preserved eggs were bought from a local market in Guilin.

Fluorescence determination and UV–Vis absorption spectra were performed on F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan) and a TU-1901 UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Beijing General Analytical Instrument Co., Ltd, China), respectively. Infrared spectrum was recorded on a Nicolet iS10 Fourier transform-infrared (FT-IR) spectrometer (Thermo Scientific, USA). The transmission electron microscopic (TEM) images were obtained on JEM-2100F TEM instrument (Jeol, Japan). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Kratos AXIS Hsi spectrometer, Britain) was used to characterize the composition of CDs. Multireception inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (MC-ICP-MS, Thermo Fisher Scientific (Bremen) Gmbh, Germany). Zeta potential and dynamic light scattering (DLS) were conducted using a Zetasizer Nano ZS90 (Malvern Instruments Ltd., Britain). The TE signals were lit up by a 635-nm laser pointer (Deli Group Co., Ltd, China). All images of TE results were taken by a smartphone and analyzed by software Image J.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of colloidal Au NPs and CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Colloidal Au NPs were synthesized according to the typical citrate reduction method. Briefly, 2.5 mL 1% (w/w) trisodium citrate solution was quickly injected into 100 mL boiling HAuCl4 (1%) under continuous stirring. The mixture was kept heating and stirring for another 20 min with the solution color changed to wine red. The obtained Au colloid solution was stored at 4 ℃ and ready for further use.

A simple hydrothermal method was used to prepare the green-emission CDs. 0.675 g urea, 0.5 mL toluene and 0.1 mL ethylenediamine were mixed in 25 mL H2O. The mixture was loaded into a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and reacted at 160 ℃ for 6 h. After cooling to ambient temperature, the solution was first filtered with 0.22 μm filter membrane and then dialyzed with a dialysis membrane (500 Da, molecular weight cutoff) for about 24 h. The obtained CDs were stored at 4 ℃ and ready to be used.

Briefly, the mixture solution of 675 nmol/L S-DNA and 225 nmol/L E-DNA in 50 mmol/L Tris buffer (pH 7.2) was first annealed at 95 ℃ for 6 min, and then incubated at 37 ℃ for 3 h to get the DNA solution. 40 μL of Pb2+ solution was added into 100 μL of the DNA solution and reacted at 37 ℃ for 30 min. Subsequently, 120 μL of this reaction solution was mixed with 500 μL Au NPs for 3 min, followed by the addition of 100 μL NaCl. The resulting solution was then ready for Pb2+ detection through dual optical channels easily. For TE method, the final solution was observed in a handmade black box with a fixed laser pointer inside. A smartphone was applied to take the TE photo, the grey value of which was analyzed by Image J and used as the measure of TE intensity. For fluorescence method, the final solution was mixed with 1 mL CDs solution for 5 min and then the fluorescence signal was detected.

A preserved egg was first cut into small pieces and ground to paste. 5 mL H2O2 and 10 mL HNO3 were added into the paste and the mixture was heated to boiling until the mixture completely turned to transparent light yellow solution with all solid particles dissolved. The obtained solution was used as the practical stock solution of Pb2+ and was diluted for the fluorescence and TE detection, which followed the same processes in section 2.3.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of synthesized Au NPs and CDs
ce:section.ce:para: CDs synthesized by the hydrothermal method were also characterized in detail. TEM image (Fig. 1C) displayed the CDs were monodispersed in spherical shape with average diameters of 7.1 ± 1.3 nm. High resolution TEM observation of Fig. 1D showed the presence of different lattice fringes in a single CD particle, due to the defects in CDs (Naik, Bhosale, & Kolekar, 2022). The well-resolved interplanar lattice spacings, calculated by Digital MicroGraph software, were around 0.22 and 0.27 nm, which corresponded to (100) and (020) facet of graphitic carbon(Wang et al., 2020; Zheng et al., 2022), respectively. Both lattice spacings were lower than the graphite lattice (0.334 nm), probably attributed to the mixed sp2 and sp3 hybridization among carbon and other atoms(Wang et al., 2022). The CDs had two UV–Vis absorption peaks at 232 nm and 280 nm (Fig. 1E), corresponding to π-π* transition of sp2 C bonds in CDs cores (Kwon, Do, Kim, Jeong, & Rhee, 2015). Three-dimensional (3D) fluorescence measurements (Fig. 1E inset) indicated that the synthesized CDs exhibited a single and narrow emission center in 3D fluorescence plots, demonstrating the narrow size distribution of CDs and homogeneous species in system. The optimal excitation and emission wavelength were 410 nm and 525 nm, respectively, with a large Stokes shift. FT-IR was used to characterize the functional groups on CDs surface (Fig. 1F). The characteristic absorptions, 1670 cm−1 for ν(CO/CN), 1501 cm−1 for ν(CO), and 3436 cm−1 for ν(OH/N H), confirming rich groups containing O an N elements on CDs surface(Jiang et al., 2023). The surface state of CDs was further investigated by XPS. The existence of abundant N elements usually rendered positive charges on CDs surface (Naik, Bhosale, & Kolekar, 2022). The high resolution spectrum of C 1s was deconvoluted into three peaks (Fig. 1G), CC/CC (285.2 eV), CN/CO (286.6 eV), and CO (289.0 eV). O 1s XPS spectrum (Fig. 1H) showed peaks of CO (531.6 eV) and CO (533.0 eV), while N 1s (Fig. 1I) had peaks of N-amide (398.8 eV) and N-pyridine (399.6 eV). The XPS results were consistent with that of FT-IR.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Feasibility of Pb2+ detection through dual optical channels of Au NPs system
ce:section.ce:para: To achieve Pb2+ detection through dual optical channels, both scattering and absorption of Au NPs were supposed to have observable change induced by addition of Pb2+, or in other words, the dispersion state of Au NPs could be greatly affected by DNAzyme with the Pb2+ cofactor. So E-DNA and S-DNA were first treated to have the specific conformation of DNAzyme, and S-DNA was then cut into free and short ssDNA in repeated cycles at the presence of Pb2+. When such reaction solution was mixed with Au NPs under high salt concentration (0.25 mol/L), the UV–Vis spectrum changed obviously compared with that of blank solution. As shown in Fig. 2 A, the blank solution had a broad absorption band from 510 to 630 nm with the color of dark blue, indicating the distinct aggregation of Au NPs. In the addition of Pb2+, the sample solution had a strong and well-defined absorption peak at 520 nm and a slight peak around 620 nm with the color of red, implying the monodispersed state of Au NPs. TEM results further verified the aggregation and disgregation of Au NPs in blank and sample solution (Fig. 2B,C), confirming the prominent change of Au NPs in dispersion amplified by Pb2+ activated DNAzyme.

Based on this result, the size of particles in solution varied from several hundred nm (aggregation) to less than 30 nm (disgregation), predicting the considerable change in scattering and the high possibility of taking scattering signal, i.e., TE signal, as quantitative measure for Pb2+ concentration. As depicted in Fig. 2D, the images of blank and sample solution showed clear light path under laser pointer irradiation, the typical TE phenomenon in colloidal solution. Since the irradiation wavelength was 635 nm, larger than most particle size, it implied the Rayleigh scattering was the main physical principle behind the Tyndall effect in our system. The mean grey value (MGV) of blank and sample images analysed by ImageJ were noticeably different, demonstrating the feasibility of Pb2+ detection through TE method. The MGV difference between blank and sample images is noted as ΔMGV.

As proved in Fig. 2A, the absorption of Au NPs in blank and sample solution changed a lot, so the overlap between absorption curve of Au NPs and emission spectrum of CDs was also different, causing distinct fluorescence intensity. To ensure the quenching effect, CDs with the emission peak at 525 nm were synthesized, nearly fully overlapped with the absorption of monodispersed Au NPs (Fig. 3 A). As a result, the presence of Pb2+ was expected to effectively quench the fluorescence of CDs, while the blank solution had little influence on CDs fluorescence (Fig. 3B). However, before the fluorescence signal of CDs was taken as the measure for Pb2+ detection, the effect of other species on CDs fluorescence in the system had to be excluded. S-DNA, E-DNA, NaCl, and Pb2+ in concentrations higher than those used in Fig. 3B were applied to study their influence on CDs fluorescence. Fig. 3C illustrated that the four species all had slight influence on fluorescence intensity, which confirmed that the change of fluorescence was caused only by Au NPs in different dispersion state induced by Pb2+. Consequently, fluorescent detection of Pb2+ based on the absorption property of Au NPs in our system was also verified.

The possible mechanism of Au NPs quenching CDs fluorescence was further explored. As mentioned before, the synthesized Au NPs were negatively charged (citrate ligands) and CDs were probably positively charged (amine groups) on surface. Zeta potential test in Fig. 3D gave the result in detail, 30.0 mV for CDs and −42 mV for Au NPs. Considering the strong electrostatic interaction between them, the reasonable pathway might be that CDs were quenched by adsorbing onto Au NPs surface and forming new complex, or they just kept close to each other, not touching. To make clear of this, the UV–Vis spectra were investigated to give the preliminary information. In the mixture solution, the characteristic absorption peaks corresponded quite well with those of monodispersed Au NPs and CDs separate solutions (Fig. 3E). It suggested the free dispersion of Au NPs and CDs and weak influence on each other in mixture. Thus, the emission energy of CDs was transferred to Au NPs at close vicinity, without forming a new complex. The comparison of fluorescence lifetime finally helped to determine the process of energy transfer. As shown in Fig. 3F, the lifetime of CDs was reduced from 4.81 ns to 3.91 ns after mixing with Au NPs. According to the FRET efficiency equation(Biskup et al., 2007) and the discussion of difference between FRET and IFE (Qin, Lu, Bian, Xiao, Zhang, & Yuan, 2019), the quenching mode of Au NPs to CDs in our system was FRET.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of experimental conditions in dual channels
ce:section.ce:para: To construct the most sensitive system responding to Au NPs dispersion change, conditions affecting absorption and scattering signals were comprehensively optimized. Based on the aforementioned process, the time and temperature for reaction between DNAzyme and Pb2+, and the concentration ratio of E-DNA to S-DNA, were the key parameters in DNAzyme reaction. Firstly, the different reaction time, 5, 10, 20, 30, 40 and 50 min, were set for DNAzyme and Pb2+. During the reaction, S-DNA was cut in cycles with the assistance of cofactor Pb2+ and the short unfolded ssDNA were released to protect Au NPs. So with the increase of reaction time from 5 to 30 min, as shown in Fig. S1A, TE intensity of sample solution gradually decreased. It was ascribed to the stronger protection of accumulated ssDNA to produce smaller size of Au NPs, and hence weaker TE signal. When reaction time was prolonged, TE intensity of sample solution remained nearly constant, indicating the protection toward Au NPs reached saturation and the increased ssDNA had little effect to dispersion of Au NPs. Conversely, TE intensity of the blank solutions at different reaction time were all similar since DNAzyme had no effect without the cofactor Pb2+. As a result, 30 min was selected as the reaction time for Pb2+ and DNAzyme, taking ΔMGV as standard. Besides, the frequently tested temperature for DNA reaction in literature, 4, 25, 30, 37, 45, and 60 ℃, were studied to find the optimum for DNAzyme reaction. Fig. S1B showed that too low and too high temperature were both negative, and ΔMGV obtained the highest only when DNAzyme reaction at 37 ℃, which was the common temperature in reaction involving DNA.

Furthermore, the concentration ratio of E-DNA to S-DNA was discussed by fluorescence method. As the leading role in DNAzyme reaction, ratio of E-DNA to S-DNA directly decided the amplification effect. Fixing the total DNA concentration at 900 nmol/L, the ratio of E-DNA to S-DNA at 3:1, 1:1, 1:2, 1:3, and 1:5, together with pure E-DNA and S-DNA, were applied to check their influence on the Au NPs and CDs system (Fig. S1C). It was found that compared with S-DNA, E-DNA itself had certain protection to Au NPs (bigger fluorescence difference ΔF), because the folded E-DNA still had free segment at both strand ends while the strand end of S-DNA hybridized to hairpin structure. So from 3:1 to 1:5, ΔF decreased as the amount of E-DNA decreased. Thereinto, as the exception of 1:3, ΔF got maximum because the cyclic reaction of S-DNA and E-DNA ran to the highest extent at this ratio, and produced the most unfolded DNA to protect Au NPs.

For TE and fluorescence detection mode, concentrations of NaCl, Au NPs and CDs were important factors to be discussed. The proper NaCl concentration could make Au NPs sensitively respond to the DNA change, not too low to induce aggregation or too high to reflect any difference. Fig. S1D showed the general trend of TE intensity was rising up in both sample and blank solutions with the increasing NaCl concentration, but the increasing rate of sample solution was slow due to the protection of free unfolded DNA. So ΔMGV was taken as standard and it rose to peak at 0.25 mol/L. Under this NaCl concentration, Au NPs concentration was also optimized. NaCl and DNA in system both worked on Au NPs and it came to the equilibrium at certain Au NPs concentration. With the dilution of the stock Au NPs solution (concentration set as 1) by eight different times, as shown in Fig. S1E, Au NPs at 0.8 acted as the optimum medium and had the maximum ΔMGV. At fixed concentrations of NaCl and Au NPs, fluorescence signal of system was dependent on CDs concentration. If CDs concentration was too high, fluorescence change induced by Au NPs was negligible compared to strong background, and if too low, the fluorescence intensity would be weak with low sensitivity. After trials with a series of dilution time, CDs concentration diluted by 6 times was found to exhibit appropriate fluorescence intensity and get the best result of ΔF (Fig. S1F).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Pb2+ detection performance of dual modes
ce:section.ce:para: Under the optimum conditions, Pb2+ detection in dual modes were carried out. For TE mode, as displayed in Fig. 4 A, the color of sample solution changed from blue to wine red as the Pb2+ concentration increasing, along with obvious decline in TE intensity. By quantitative analysis of these TE images by ImageJ, the linear relationship between logarithm of Pb2+ concentration and the ΔMGV of corresponding solution was established. The linear range was 2.4 × 10-13 ∼ 9.5 × 10-9 mol/L (R2 = 0.9970), with the detection limit of 0.11 pmol/L, which is much lower than the recommended residue limit of Pb2+ in drinking water (48 nmol/L) stipulated by WHO. This might be the lowest detection limit of Pb2+ achieved by a non-instrumental analysis method to the best of our knowledge. As a novel non-instrumental colorimetric technique, TE detection was accomplished only with tools available in daily life, laser pointer and smart phone, which made it competitive in point-of-need usage. Since Au NPs in sample solution with different Pb2+ also had color change, TE result was compared with that of traditional UV–Vis method. With the increase of Pb2+, more unfolded ssDNA was released to protect Au NPs, and the absorption at 520 nm became stronger while absorption at 650 nm became weaker (Fig. 4B). Taking A520/A650 as the quantitative measure, the calibration curve of UV–Vis method was illustrated in Fig. 4B inset, comparing with that of TE method. It was clearly seen that for UV–Vis result, the linear range was narrower (2.4 × 10-13 ∼ 2.4 × 10-9mol/L), and the sensitivity was also much lower (curve slope), indicating the admirable detection performance of TE mode.

For fluorescence mode, analysis of Pb2+ in the Au NPs and CDs system amplified by DNAzyme was investigated. Fig. 4C,D illustrated that the fluorescence intensity of CDs was gradually quenched with the increasing concentration of Pb2+. There was a good linear calibration plot of fluorescence intensity and logarithm of Pb2+ concentration ranging from 2.0 × 10-14 ∼ 8.0 × 10-10 mol/L (R2 = 0.9934), with the detection limit of 6.7 fmol/L, which was also way lower than 48 nmol/L.

To evaluate the selectivity of dual modes, the Pb2+ sample (100 pmol/L) and 15 other metal ions (Ag+, Hg2+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Co2+, Cu2+, Cd2+, Mn2+, Zn2+, Ni2+, Er3+, Fe3+, Al3+, Bi3+, Cr6+; 1 nmol/L each) were tested separately under the identical conditions. The TE interference study showed that weak ΔMGV value was obtained from Pb2+ (ca. 50), while similar strong ΔMGV (ca. 100) were obtained from blank solution and 15 interfering metal ions (Fig. 4E). Meanwhile, the fluorescence interference data manifested that only Pb2+ was able to achieve the quenched intensity and other metal ions had litter effect to fluorescence (Fig. 4F). This was due to firstly the negligible influence of interfering metal ions on our synthesized Au NPs and CDs, and secondly the specific activation of Pb2+ to chosen DNAzyme. The result proved that the dual-mode strategy exhibited admirable selectivity toward Pb2+.

To demonstrate the accuracy and practicality of the proposed strategy, the Pb2+ concentration in practical sample, the Chinese traditional food–preserved egg, was determined, as well as spiked sample with known concentration of Pb2+. Before analysis, the preserved egg sample was pretreated by digestion process with boiling H2O2 and HNO3, as described in Section 2.4, to get totally dissolved sample solution. After adjusting pH to around 2.4, the diluted sample solution was determined by TE and fluorescence methods, and the results were listed in Table 1 . It was found by both methods that there was Pb2+ residual in preserved egg, which was confirmed by the result of ICP. The Pb2+ concentration in preserved egg was calculated to be around 0.12 μg/g, which was far lower than Chinese hygienic standard for egg product (2 μg/g, GB 2479–2003), indicating it was qualified food for consumption. The recovery of the spiked samples ranged from 98.21% to 112.12% and the RSD were between 1.84 and 2.48%, proving the practicability of this dual-mode strategy in real sample. For comparison, indicators including detection method, linear range and portability achieved in other literature were summarized in Table S1. The results showed that our proposed detection strategy had satisfactory detection range, much lower detection limit and even portability, manifesting the good applicability of this technique.

A dual-mode sensing platform based on the scattering and absorption properties of Au NPs enhanced by DNAzyme was constructed for the ultrasensitive detection of Pb2+. By triggering the cyclic release of free unfolded ssDNA, Pb2+ was related to the aggregation state change of Au NPs and hence was evaluated by the corresponding optical change, which in our system were translated to the TE and fluorescence (with the introduction of CDs) signals. Through the discussion to the detection process, it was found that Rayleigh scattering was the main physical principle of produced TE, and the mechanism of CDs quenched by Au NPs was FRET. Parameters affecting the two modes were studied in detail and the detection limit of both modes were pushed down to almost fmol/L level, though the TE method only involving a laser pointer and a smart phone. Furthermore, the Pb2+ detection in real sample preserved egg exhibited satisfactory performance, demonstrating the feasibility of the sensing platform in practical application, including environmental and food safety monitoring. Compared with single signal, the dual-mode managed to eliminate potential error and expand the detection range. Besides, applying more optical properties of Au NPs in diverse designs might open more channels for analysis.

Wenju Yan: Conceptualization, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Xuefei Qin: Data curation, Investigation. Xueqing Sang: Data curation, Investigation. Xueying Zhou: Writing – review & editing. Yanying Zheng: Data curation. Yali Yuan: Conceptualization, Methodology, Supervision. Yun Zhang: Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 22364011), Central Government-Guided Local Science and Technology Development Project (No. GuikeZY20198006), and Guangxi Natural Science Foundation (2020GXNSFBA297116).
